statement.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer which is the number of nodes in the linked list. This is followed by the data of each node in the linked list, one per line. The last line contains -1 to indicate the end of the linked list.
statement.outputFormat: The data of each node in the linked list, one per line.

constraints.description: number of nodes in the linked list

examples.explanation: The linked list is 1 -> 2 -> 3 -> 4 -> 5. The data of each node is printed.
The linked list is 10 -> 20 -> 30. The data of each node is printed.

hints.text: Start by initializing a variable to store the current node and another variable to store the head node of the linked list.
Use a while loop to iterate through the linked list. In each iteration, update the current node to the next node of the previous node (i.e., current = previous.next).
Inside the while loop, print the data of the current node.
After the while loop ends, print a final line to indicate the end of the linked list.
To ensure the loop runs until the head node is null (indicating the end of the list), check if the current node is null before starting the loop.

editorial.title: Introduction to Singly Linked List
editorial.intuition: The problem requires printing the data of all nodes in a singly linked list. A brute force approach would involve traversing the linked list node by node, which is correct but slow for large lists.
editorial.bruteForce: The brute force approach involves iterating through each node in the linked list, starting from the head, and printing the data of each node. This approach is correct but has a time complexity of O(N), where N is the number of nodes in the linked list, and a space complexity of O(1) if we don't consider the space required for the output.
editorial.betterSolution: However, there's an observation that can unlock an optimal approach: we can simply iterate through the linked list once and print the data of each node as we go.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution involves creating a linked list from the given input and then traversing the list to print the data of each node. This approach has a known time complexity of O(N) and space complexity of O(N) due to the linked list structure.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the optimal solution can be proven by induction. The base case is when N = 1, in which case we simply create a linked list with one node and print its data. For the inductive step, assume that the solution works for N = k, where k is a positive integer. Then, for N = k + 1, we create a linked list with k + 1 nodes and print the data of each node by traversing the list. This shows that the solution works for all positive integers N.
editorial.dryRun: A dry run with a sample input, such as N = 3 and data = [1, 2, 3], helps verify that the optimal solution works correctly.
commonMistakes: Not properly handling the head of the linked list
Forgetting to update the current node pointer while traversing the list
Not considering the space complexity of the linked list structure
Incorrectly assuming the time complexity of the brute force approach
Not verifying the solution with sample inputs
Overcomplicating the solution with unnecessary data structures
